retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation may manage symptoms at home or need urgent medical care depending on their symptom severity and risk factors for fatal asthma. These risk factors include prior life-threatening exacerbations, exacerbations despite glucocorticoid use, more than 1 asthma-related hospitalization or 3 emergency room visits in the past year, and comorbidities such as cardiovascular or chronic lung disease. Immediate medical attention is warranted for patients showing significant breathlessness, inability to speak beyond short phrases, reliance on accessory muscles, or peak flow measurements at 50% or less of their baseline measurement.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a peak flow value of 50% or lower despite administering a bronchodilator or continuing to worsen should seek immediate medical care while continuing to administer their fast-acting bronchodilator.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma (Nursing) -- Medical Management -- Other/Long Term
retrieved_docs.content: Weight loss, smoking cessation, occupational change, and self-monitoring are all important in preventing disease progression and reducing the number of acute attacks.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation may manage symptoms at home or require urgent medical care, depending on severity and risk factors for fatal asthma. Urgent medical attention is needed for those with marked shortness of breath, inability to speak more than short phrases, use of accessory muscles, mental status changes, or at high risk of fatal exacerbation, which requires urgent medical attention. Risk factors that predict a fatal asthma exacerbation include: Previous life-threatening exacerbation Exacerbation despite OCS use Multiple emergency room visits (3 or more) or hospitalizations (more than 1) in the last year Use of more than 1 SABA MDI per month Food allergies Chronic heart or lung disease Medication nonadherence Psychosocial stresssors

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Asthma Severity
retrieved_docs.content: Routine follow-up every 1 to 6 months is necessary to ensure adequate symptom management. Upon reevaluation, patients facing inadequate asthma symptom management, exacerbations necessitating systemic glucocorticoids, or those at high risk of exacerbation on their current therapy level should escalate to the next level of therapy. Therapy adjustments proceed incrementally until symptoms are adequately managed. After maintaining control for 3 to 6 months, clinicians may consider gradual therapy reduction following the stepwise protocols outlined by GINA or NAEPP guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who can be sent home from the office should have their controller medications advanced in 1 step. In addition, it is essential to review the correct use of their inhaler, discuss trigger avoidance strategies, ensure they have an asthma action plan, and emphasize the importance of adhering to their controller medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma (Nursing) -- Nursing Management -- Management of Acute Exacerbations
retrieved_docs.content: Initial management of a child who presents to the emergency department with an acute asthma exacerbation includes bronchodilators and steroids. Albuterol:

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Acute Exacerbations
retrieved_docs.content: Correcting severe hypoxemia through the application of oxygen and repetitive treatment with short-acting beta2 agonists Rapid reversal of airflow obstruction Reduction of the risk of relapse Anyone with peak expiratory flow below 50% needs immediate medical care.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Office management is similar to home management, with the addition that according to GINA guidelines, all patients with oxygen saturation below 90% should receive oxygen to maintain saturation above 92% or 95% for pregnant individuals. Albuterol treatment can be administered via an MDI or nebulizer, with a dosage of 4 to 8 puffs or 2.5 to 5 mg every 20 minutes for 1 hour, respectively. Research comparing the efficacy of an MDI combined with a valved-holding chamber to nebulizer delivery, both administering the same β-agonist but with significantly lower doses via MDI, demonstrates similar enhancements in lung function and risk reduction for hospitalization. [33] [34] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma (Nursing) -- Medical Management -- Surgical
retrieved_docs.content: There is no surgical input into the management of typical asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Emergency Department Care
retrieved_docs.content: Within the first hour, patients should receive 3 treatments of an inhaled SABA, such as albuterol, via a nebulizer or MDI, followed by repeat dosing every 1 to 4 hours. In addition to a SABA, patients with severe asthma exacerbations should also receive inhaled ipratropium, a short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA), at a dosage of 500 µg by nebulization or 4 to 8 puffs by MDI, every 20 minutes for 3 doses, and then hourly as needed for up to 3 hours. Current guidelines recommend discontinuing SAMA therapy once the patient requires hospital admission, except in specific cases such as refractory asthma requiring treatment in the intensive care unit, concurrent treatment with monoamine oxidase inhibitors due to potential increased toxicity from sympathomimetic therapy due to impaired drug metabolism, presence of COPD with an asthmatic component, or asthma triggered by β-blocker therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Asthma Severity
retrieved_docs.content: Every patient should have access to a bronchodilator with a rapid onset of action. Traditionally, this has been a short-acting β-agonist (SABA) such as albuterol. However, GINA recommends a low-dose glucocorticoid/formoterol inhaler, such as 80 to 160 mcg budesonide/4.5 mcg formoterol inhaled by mouth 1 or 2 times daily,  for asthma symptoms. Notably, this is an off-label indication for this preparation.

retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Treatment / Management -- Allergic Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Sympathomimetic β-adrenergic bronchodilators are used to abort acute attacks and for long-term management. Albuterol, metaproterenol, pirbuterol, and isoetharine are selective β-adrenergic bronchodilators administered via aerosol inhalation. Theophylline is a potent bronchodilator combined with sympathomimetic medication and can be administered 250 to 500 mg during an acute asthma attack. Epinephrine can be given subcutaneously in a 0.2 to 0.5 mL dose. Glucocorticoids are effective in treating allergic asthma; a typical dose is 30 to 60 mg. However, glucocorticoids should be reserved for asthma refractory to other therapies. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Emergency Department Care
retrieved_docs.content: As with outpatient management, patients also receive glucocorticoids equivalent to 40 to 60 mg of prednisone daily for 5 to 7 days. A systematic review reveals no difference between a higher dose and a longer course when compared to a lower dose with a shorter course of prednisone or prednisolone. [36] Oral and intravenous glucocorticoids have equivalent effects when administered in comparable doses. Intravenous steroids are necessary for patients with impending or actual respiratory arrest or who are intolerant of oral glucocorticoids. Some clinicians administer higher doses of glucocorticoids for severe asthma exacerbations based on their expert opinion and concern that a lower dose might be insufficient in a critically ill patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: If oral glucocorticoids are unavailable, intramuscular steroids such as triamcinolone suspension (40 mg/mL) 60 to 100 mg can be an alternative. However, it is noteworthy that intramuscular glucocorticoids have a delayed onset of action of 12 to 36 hours. Patients meeting certain criteria such as a respiratory rate of 30 breaths per minute, a heart rate of more than 120 bpm, a continued peak flow of less than 50% predicted, oxygen saturation of less than 90%, or the inability to speak in full sentences should be transferred to the emergency department.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients whose symptoms improve after administering a bronchodilator and whose peak flow returns to 80% of their baseline or better can continue to manage their symptoms at home. Oral glucocorticoids equivalent to 40 to 60 mg prednisone daily for 5 to 7 days are warranted for the following patients: Those experiencing recurrent symptoms over the following 1 to 2 days. Those whose peak flow remains less than 80% of their normal baseline (high-dose ICSs are an alternative). If they do not improve after 1 to 3 doses of a fast-acting bronchodilator. If they have recently completed a course in OCS. Those who are on a maximal dose of controller medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: swelling and bronchoconstriction, highlighting its potential therapeutic role in histamine‑mediated conditions. This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑asthmatic therapy involves the corticosteroid beclomethasone, which is classified as an inhaled steroid agent (ISA) and is used to manage airway disease. Beclomethasone specifically targets inflammation (الالتهاب), reducing inflammatory processes in the respiratory tract and thereby improving asthma control. This highlights beclomethasone’s central role as an anti‑asthmatic medication that treats inflammatory airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1344
retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 700
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics” comprises several specific inhaled combination products that pair a β‑adrenergic bronchodilator with an inhaled corticosteroid to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory therapy for obstructive airway diseases. Included in this class are albuterol + beclomethasone drug combinations, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. Each of these formulations is a member of the adrenergic‑corticosteroid combination category, distinguishing them from inhaled therapies that contain anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights their shared pharmacologic strategy of combining a rapid‑acting or long‑acting β‑agonist with a corticosteroid, which is central to modern asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: clinicians to select the most appropriate steroid formulation for upper‑airway (allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis) and lower‑airway (asthma, COPD) inflammatory conditions, optimizing therapeutic efficacy and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 0 -- Community 5
retrieved_docs.content: (akutní nemoc) is a contraindication for the use of long‑acting β2‑agonists such as salmeterol and formoterol, as their pharmacologic profile is unsuitable for rapidly evolving symptoms; in such settings clinicians prefer short‑acting bronchodilators. This hierarchical organization and the noted contraindications are essential for safe prescribing and effective management of respiratory conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 13
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the community’s knowledge delineates how anticholinergic and adrenergic agents are organized by selectivity, route of administration, and combination therapy, and it clarifies their specific indications and contraindications in acute inflammatory respiratory conditions such as acute bronchitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1234
retrieved_docs.content: Budesonide, a glucocorticoid employed to reduce airway inflammation, is one of the two active components of the fixed‑dose combination inhaler budesonide/formoterol; this medication pairs budesonide with the long‑acting β2‑agonist formoterol to deliver both anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator effects for the management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 182
retrieved_docs.content: a β‑adrenergic agonist, providing both bronchodilatory (via isoproterenol) and anti‑inflammatory (via cromolyn) actions. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a systemic corticosteroid, is indicated for the treatment of both حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (asthmatic exacerbations) and الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse or shock). Its anti‑inflammatory and hemodynamic support properties complement the mast‑cell‑stabilizing effects of cromolyn in acute severe asthma and in states of hemodynamic instability. Together, these relationships highlight a therapeutic network in which cromolyn, alone or in combination with albuterol, isoproterenol, or other agents, modulates mast‑cell‑mediated inflammation, prevents exercise‑induced bronchospasm, and must be used judiciously in severe asthma, while hydrocortisone sodium succinate provides broader anti‑inflammatory and circulatory support for severe asthma attacks and shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: long‑acting β₂‑agonists such as salmeterol are contraindicated in acute bronchitis. Together, ipratropium bromide (muscarinic antagonism) and the various selective β₂‑agonists—including formoterol, salmeterol, and others—provide complementary bronchodilatory options, with their distinct mechanisms, classifications, elimination pathways, therapeutic indications and contraindications guiding clinicians in the management of obstructive airway diseases and acute bronchitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1209
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics is a therapeutic class that comprises several fixed‑dose inhaler formulations used to treat obstructive airway diseases. Within this class, the albuterol + beclomethasone drug combination, the budesonide / formoterol combination, and the formoterol / mometasone combination are all members. Each of these products pairs a rapid‑acting β2‑adrenergic agonist (albuterol or formoterol) with an inhaled corticosteroid (beclomethasone, budesonide, or mometasone) to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects while deliberately excluding anticholinergic agents. This grouping highlights the shared pharmacologic strategy of combining adrenergic bronchodilators with corticosteroids to improve symptom control and reduce exacerbations in patients with asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics is a therapeutic class that includes the drug combinations albuterol, beclomethasone and formoterol / mometasone. These combination inhalers pair a β2‑adrenergic agonist with an inhaled corticosteroid to provide both bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects while deliberately excluding anticholinergic agents. By grouping albuterol‑beclomethasone and formoterol‑mometasone together, the class underscores a common pharmacologic strategy used in the management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, where synergistic control of airway obstruction and inflammation improves patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 0 -- Community 0
retrieved_docs.content: in acute disease (“akutní nemoc”): both Formoterol and salmeterol (and, by extension, other long‑acting agents) should be avoided in patients with acute illness. These relationships delineate the classification of β‑adrenergic agents by receptor selectivity, route of administration, combination formulation, and therapeutic contraindications, thereby guiding clinicians in the selection and safe use of bronchodilators for obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network of inhaled, intranasal, and systemic glucocorticoid therapies used for obstructive airway diseases and related conditions. Adrenergic inhalants combined with corticosteroids (excluding anticholinergics) form a therapeutic class that includes fixed‑dose products such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone (Momesalic), and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate; budesonide, Momesalic, and fluticasone furoate are the corticosteroid components of these combinations. The same corticosteroids—budesonide, Momesalic, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—appear in topical nasal preparations and in the class of glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease, which also encompasses systemic glucocorticoid derivatives such as hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and the child agent triamcinolone diacetate. These agents are employed with long‑acting β‑agonists (formoterol, vilanterol) or short‑acting β‑agonists (albuterol) to treat asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and allergic rhinitis. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, administered intravenously, is additionally indicated for acute bronchial or asthmatic exacerbations and for circulatory collapse, illustrating the therapeutic continuum from topical anti‑inflammatory inhalation to systemic emergency management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2860
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excl. anticholinergics is a therapeutic class of inhaled respiratory agents that pair a β‑adrenergic bronchodilator with a corticosteroid or another adjunctive medication while specifically excluding anticholinergic components. Within this class, the albuterol, beclomethasone drug combinations and the budesonide / formoterol formulation are recognized members. Both albuterol‑beclomethasone and budesonide‑formoterol provide dual action: rapid bronchodilation from the adrenergic component and sustained anti‑inflammatory control from the corticosteroid, making them valuable options for the long‑term management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 101
retrieved_docs.content: adrenergic and anticholinergic inhalants, corticosteroid combinations, antiallergic agents, systemic bronchodilators, and biologic monoclonal antibodies—provide a structured framework that supports clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic strategies for patients with obstructive airway diseases and related inflammatory conditions.
